post: In the summer of 1775, the Continental Congress tried one more time. The Olive Branch Petition was an attempt to preserve the benefits of being British. The Royal Navy's protection of American ships was chief among them. Colonists had genuinely valued membership in the empire. They weren't looking for a reason to leave. George III declared the colonies to be in open rebellion. Parliament approved the Prohibitory Act in December 1775. The Act cut off all commerce with the colonies and declared every American ship enemy property, making it lawful for the Royal Navy to attack and seize vessels it had previously protected. Hannah Nolan, PhD Candidate at the University of Maryland, explores why this backfired so completely. John Hancock put it plainly: "The making all our vessels lawful prize doesn't look like a reconciliation." The Declaration of Independence cited the Act as proof that George III had forsaken his duty to protect his subjects. In doing so, patriots asserted something foundational: governments owe their citizens the ability to move freely and criticize their leaders without fear of reprisal. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

Before he became the hero of the American Revolution, he was a 22-year-old soldier who made a rash decision in the Pennsylvania wilderness, and accidentally started the French and Indian War. That's the story of George Washington that students don't often learn- a story of how failure became a great teacher, shaping his character as a leader. Young Washington (in theaters July 3) brings this real, human, and vivid story to life. This fall, David Bobb and BRI are developing curriculum-aligned lesson plans and discussion guides tied to the film, reaching more than 90,000 educators with a digital hub for classroom access and student engagement. As David told The 74 Media: this goes beyond memorizing dates. It's about sparking curiosity. Thank you to Daniels Fund for making these materials possible. Full story:  https://bit.ly/4v1V5QO  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #K12Education  #YoungWashington

July 4th is 9 days away. If you've been looking for a way to celebrate the 250th in the most traditionally American way, David Bobb and Tony Williams wrote the book. Divided Over the Declaration traces 250 years of Americans debating what the Declaration of Independence means: from the enslaved people who made claims to freedom after it was it was signed, to Abigail Adams demanding the Founders remember the women, to Frederick Douglass insisting America be held to its own promise. Support BRI. Receive the book. Mark America's 250th. $75: Divided Over the Declaration, delivered to you $150: A copy signed by both David and Tony (our pick) $250: Signed copy + exclusive live webinar with the authors (very limited) Signed copies are going. Webinar seats are nearly gone. Your gift sustains BRI's growing teacher network, the Civics Innovation Lab, and the work that doesn't stop on July 5th. Get your book today:  https://lnkd.in/emzE8M3E

87% of progressives. 84% of MAGA Republicans. That's the percentage of both groups who think the U. S. puts too little emphasis on civic education. A new NBC News poll of 3,000 Americans found that 80% believe the United States puts too little emphasis on civic education. And that number holds across ideological lines, across generations, across every demographic divide the poll tested. In the most polarized political environment in living memory, Americans from opposite ends of the spectrum are looking at the same classroom and reaching the same conclusion: students aren't getting enough. BRI has spent 26 years trying to fix exactly that, with 6,000+ free, scholar-reviewed civics and history resources for teachers in all 50 states, serving 9 million students. 10 days before America's 250th birthday, the poll is a reminder: the civic crisis isn't partisan. And the solution doesn't have to be either. The full article is worth a read 👇  https://nbcnews.to/4eCc6w1  #CivicEducation

The Declaration of Independence was released in July 1776. The Revolutionary War had started 15 months earlier. That timing is central to Grievance 23. By the time Jefferson drafted the Declaration, the colonists were documenting a break that had already occurred, in blood, at Lexington and Concord in April 1775. Grievance 23 charges King George III with waging war against the colonists and withdrawing the protection they were owed. The specific action at its center is the Prohibitory Act. Dr. Matthew Goetz of the White House Historical Association explains John Adams's reading of that moment: Adams believed the Prohibitory Act had already effectively declared independence, not by the colonists, but by the British government. By withdrawing protection while already at war with the colonies, the Crown had abdicated the governing relationship it was supposed to uphold. What remained was 2 parties in open conflict, with the governing relationship already severed. The Declaration made official what British action had already produced. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M New episodes every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

Last week, BRI had a party to celebrate America's 250th birthday and the release of Divided Over the Declaration, the new book by David Bobb and Tony Williams about 250 years of debate over the meaning and legacy of the Declaration of Independence. David and Tony signed copies, guests loved the photo booth, and BRI's best-dressed employee, Echo the Golden Retriever, stole the show. In between all of that, something happened that's easy to miss in photos but hard to miss in person: a room full of people who believe the story of America's founding is still worth telling, and our principles still worth arguing! Thank you for everyone who joined us for a lovely evening! Divided Over the Declaration is out now:  https://bit.ly/4oL7HKy  #A250  #DividedOvertheDeclaration

The phrase "in all cases whatsoever" is what makes Grievance 22 so alarming. Parliament declared itself empowered to legislate for the colonies without exception, while simultaneously suspending the colonial legislatures that had represented colonists for generations. Sasha Rickard of MIT Concourse explains Grievance 22 through John Locke's framewor. Locke argued that anyone who attempts to gain absolute power over another "must be understood to be acting aggressively," and that resistance is justified even before harm occurs. Simply by claiming unlimited legislative authority, Parliament had already committed what Locke called an act of war. Britain had claimed unlimited power and dismantled the only institution capable of checking it. For colonists reasoning from Lockean principles, this wasn't a policy dispute. It was a government that had declared war on the governed, and justified the governed in responding. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/4uOsIoM  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #JohnLocke

The Massachusetts Bay Colonial Charter was signed in 1629. For 145 years, it served as the contractual foundation of colonial self-governance, defining the relationship between the colony and the Crown, establishing how government would operate, and securing rights colonists had come to understand as theirs. In 1774, Parliament rewrote it without asking. The Massachusetts Government Act made unilateral changes to a charter that had been renegotiated once before  in 1691, following the Glorious Revolution  through a process that reflected the mutual relationship between colony and Crown. That precedent made the 1774 Act more than a policy change. It was a breach of the governing framework itself. Kelsa Pelletiere of the University of Mississippi explains why Grievance 21 targets this act specifically: the consent of the governed isn't just a principle that applies to individual laws. It applies to the structures of government itself. When Parliament changed those structures without colonial approval, it signaled that colonial self-governance had never been truly secure, that it existed only at the Crown's pleasure. Episode 21 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory
